title: The Front Men of Wall Street: The Role of CDO Collateral Managers in the CDO Boom and Bust
abstract: I study the incentives of the collateral managers who selected securities for ABS CDOs—securitizations that figured prominently in the financial crisis. Specialized managers without other businesses that could suffer negative reputational consequences invested in low-quality securities underwritten by the CDO's arranger. These securities performed significantly worse than observationally similar securities. Managers investing in these securities were rewarded with additional collateral management assignments. Diversified managers who did assemble CDOs suffered negative reputational consequences during the crisis: institutional investors withdrew from their mutual funds. Overall, the results are consistent with a quid pro quo between collateral managers and CDO underwriters.

article.introduction:
Structured products such as private-labelresidential mortgage-backed securities (RMBS) and collateralized debt obligations (CDOs) were at the heart of the recent credit boom and the 2007 to 2009 financial crisis. Given the role of these securities in the crisis, researchers have analyzed the incentives of the loan originators,1credit ratings agencies,2and underwriters3bringing these securities to the market. The literature has also started to explore the demand for securitizations, pointing to the reliance on credit ratings and regulatory arbitrage on the one hand4and the neglect of risk on the other hand.5
Most of the demand for the non-AAA-rated tranches of private-label RMBS, however, came not from end investors but from CDOs that invested in asset-backed securities (ABS CDOs). During the 2002 to 2007 period, issuance of non-AAA-rated private-label RMBS amounted to about $661 billion.6Over the same period, $860 billion of ABS CDOs, which invested primarily in non-AAA-rated RMBS, were issued.7For the vast majority of these CDOs, the ultimate responsibility for the selection of the underlying collateral rested with independent asset management firms called CDO collateral managers. Several years after the crisis, the academic literature still offers almost no systematic evidence on the identity, incentives, and role played by these asset management firms.
In the meantime, a popular view that has emerged in the wake of the crisis and that has been popularized by Michael Lewis in his bookThe Big Shortis that collateral managers acted as front men for the investment banks underwriting the CDOs: “investors felt better buying a Merrill Lynch CDO if it didn't appear to be run by Merrill Lynch” (Lewis (2010, p. 143)). According to this front men view, collateral managers engaged in a quid pro quo with investment banks: in return for being handed CDO management assignments, collateral managers had their CDOs invest in low-quality RMBS collateral that the investment banks were unable to sell to anyone else. The front men view has been at the core of a number of recent SEC administrative proceedings and private lawsuits against collateral managers, claiming breach of contract and misrepresentation of who would make collateral selection decisions.8Perhaps the most prominent of these lawsuits are those involving deals sponsored by Magnetar Capital.
As described in a popular NPR report,9the “Magnetar Trade” involved the hedge fund sponsoring a number of CDOs by investing in their equity tranches and simultaneously betting against them by entering into credit default swaps (CDS) on the CDOs' collateral securities and higher-rated tranches. At the same time, Magnetar pressured the CDO collateral managers into purchasing risky bonds for their portfolios, thereby increasing the probability that the CDOs would fail and that Magnetar's short bets would pay off.
Not all collateral managers were willing to abdicate their responsibility for the selection of collateral, however. Many worried about the possibility of negative reputational effects for their non-CDO businesses. One example is Ischus Capital Management, the CDO management subsidiary of Resource America, a publicly traded asset manager. In September 2006, Ischus and Magnetar sought to work together on a deal called Hydrus 2006-1. In an email exchange with Magnetar, Ischus appeared to be uncomfortable with the portfolio of securities suggested by Magnetar, saying “we will not assemble a portfolio we are not proud of.”10As a result of Ischus's unwillingness to invest in collateral it considered too risky, negotiations between Ischus and Magnetar fell apart and the CDO was never issued.
Based on the above discussion, the front men narrative leads to four key predictions. First, underwriters rewarded managers willing to invest in low-quality collateral with future collateral management assignments. Second, because the franchise value of their other businesses was at stake, diversified managers were less likely to engage in bad deal-making in an effort to attract deal flow. This implies, in turn, that diversified managers lost market share over time, and that the CDOs of diversified managers performed better than deals of specialized managers. Third, a key driver of specialized managers' underperformance was their willingness to cater to CDO underwriters by purchasing the banks' unsold inventory of subprime RMBS and CDOs. And fourth, diversified managers who did assemble ABS CDOs suffered negative reputational consequences during the crisis.
This paper studies the identity, incentives, and performance of the collateral managers of ABS CDOs issued during the 2002 to 2007 period and provides evidence that supports the main predictions of the front men narrative. I start by examining the relationship between the characteristics and performance of a manager's past CDOs and his future deal volume. I find that managers whose past CDOs perform poorly get more deal assignments. While CDO performance was not observable in real time, the results are consistent with the idea that investment banks knew which collateral managers had invested in lower quality collateral, which then performed worse ex post. Consistent with this interpretation, the negative effect of past performance on future deal activity works largely through the collateral manager's investment in securities underwritten by the CDO's arranger and securities that Magnetar Capital bet against.
Given these incentives, which managers were more likely to engage in bad deal-making to attract deal flow? The front men narrative predicts that managers with low franchise value should be more willing to engage in such behavior. It is worth noting that collateral managers generally have small stakes, if any, in the CDOs that they manage and that a large fraction of their management fees is in the form of upfront and senior fees that do not depend much on the performance of the CDO. Therefore, from the collateral manager's perspective, the major risk in this setting is not so much financial risk as reputational risk that stems from knowingly investing in bad collateral that the investment banks were unable to sell to anyone else or that someone else wanted to bet against.
My proxy for a collateral manager's franchise value considerations is the manager'sCDO Share—the manager's ABS CDOs as a fraction of their total assets under management (AUM). Managers with lowCDO Shareshould be concerned about the potential negative effects of risk-taking in CDOs on the franchise value of their other businesses. Comparing similar deals that closed at the same time, I find that going from 0% to 100%CDO Shareis associated with 6.6% lower internal rate of return (IRR).
Although consistent with diversified managers engaging in less risk-taking due to franchise value considerations, the underperformance of specialized managers in and of itself is also consistent with a number of alternative explanations. I consider and provide evidence against three main alternatives. First, the results are not driven by differences in direct incentives in the form of management fees or equity stakes in the CDOs. Second, the results cannot be explained by differences in employees' ability or experience. Third, the decision to specialize is obviously not random: some managers may have chosen to specialize because they were more optimistic about the subprime RMBS collateral that their CDOs invested in. If this were the case, we would expect to see specialized managers tilting their compensation toward performance-sensitive fees and investing in the equity tranches of their deals. The evidence points in the other direction, however—over the course of the CDO boom, the compensation of specialized managers became less, not more, sensitive to performance.
What did specialized managers do differently that made their CDOs perform poorly? I show that there were three noteworthy differences between CDOs of specialized and diversified managers. First, within a given asset class and rating, specialized managers invested in riskier, higher yielding securities. Second, they invested a larger share of the CDO's portfolio in the collateral securities that Magnetar Capital bet against. Third, they invested in collateral securities underwritten by the CDO's arranger. Furthermore, whenever specialized managers purchased collateral securities underwritten by the investment bank arranging the CDO, such collateral securities performed significantly worse than securities in the same asset class and with the same credit rating and vintage. However, the identity of the collateral underwriter does not matter for deals managed by diversified managers. Thus, the types of CDOs that specialized managers put together were exactly the types of deals that investment banks rewarded with future deal assignments.
Finally, I examine the reputational consequences of CDO management. Looking at flows into mutual funds, I find that institutional investors withdrew significantly more capital from mutual funds offered by asset management firms that also managed ABS CDOs. These outflows cannot be explained by differences in fund performance or exposure to subprime RMBS, and are instead consistent with negative reputational consequences of CDO management. Over the course of the crisis period, August 2007 to December 2008, institutional outflows from the affected funds cumulate to 10.2% of assets.
Overall, the constellation of evidence presented in this paper is consistent with the idea that collateral managers catered to CDO underwriters by investing in low-quality collateral. Furthermore, the evidence suggests that stand-alone specialized managers were more likely to engage in bad deal-making. Concerned about the negative effects on the franchise value of their other businesses, diversified managers strove to be more selective. Nevertheless, competition from specialized managers could have created a race to the bottom and forced diversified managers to lower their standards as well.11For example, despite its concerns about the reputational risks involved, State Street agreed to manage a Magnetar-sponsored CDO called Carina because “there were other managers who were dying to do this deal” (Eisinger and Bernstein (2010)).12Although the decline in deal performance across vintages is certainly consistent with a race to the bottom, it is difficult to convincingly rule out alternative explanations based on time-series changes in collateral quality. Thus, my results can best speak to cross-sectional differences in the incentives and performance of diversified versus specialized managers.
My paper contributes to a growing literature on the conflicts of interest in securitization. Keys et al. (2009,2010) and Keys, Seru, and Vig (2012) study how securitization affects lenders' screening decisions. Griffin, Lowery, and Saretto (2014) look at the performance of ABS underwritten by different investment banks and find that securities, including CDOs, underwritten by more reputable banks perform worse. Griffin and Tang (2012) and Griffin, Nickerson, and Tang (2013) study the credit ratings of CDOs. Griffin and Tang (2012) show that a major credit rating agency made subjective adjustments to its model, whose effect was to increase the fraction of the deal-rated AAA, while Griffin, Nickerson, and Tang (2013) find evidence of catering by rating agencies to CDOs. My paper studies conflicts of interest faced by CDO collateral managers and provides evidence of catering by managers to CDO underwriters.
The paper is also related to the literature on the demand for securitizations. Erel, Nadauld, and Stulz (2013) find that bank holdings of highly rated tranches are a by-product of their securitization activity, while Merrill, Nadauld, and Strahan (2014) study the incentives of insurance companies to invest in structured products. Chernenko, Hanson, and Sunderam (2016) suggest that the lack of negative firsthand experience in credit markets was an important driver of mutual fund managers' optimism and investment in AAA-rated subprime RMBS and CDOs. In this paper, I point out that ABS CDOs were the primary source of demand for non-AAA-rated tranches of private-label RMBS, with the collateral managers being in charge of portfolio selection. The paper shows how the direct and indirect incentives of collateral managers affected their demand for securitizations.
More broadly, this paper contributes to our understanding of the costs and benefits of specialization in asset management. Because they do not have any other businesses that could be adversely affected by such risk-taking, specialized managers may engage in greater risk-taking than diversified managers (Keeley (1990), Kacperczyk and Schnabl (2013)).
The rest of the paper is organized as follows. SectionI.provides additional institutional background on collateral managers, their compensation, and their incentives. SectionII.describes the sample of CDOs and data sources used in the paper. SectionIII.reports the results. SectionIV.concludes.
article.I. Institutional Background:
CDOs are special purpose entities that issue notes, mostly rated AAA, to invest in a portfolio of bonds, loans, and CDS. Noteholders are paid from the interest and principal generated by these underlying collateral assets. Because CDOs themselves are legal shells, selection of collateral is outsourced to independent asset management firms. Similarly to a mutual fund or a hedge fund entering into an investment advisory agreement with an investment manager, CDOs enter into collateral management agreements with asset management firms called CDO collateral managers. The collateral management agreement spells out the responsibilities, compensation, and liability of the collateral manager, as well as the conditions under which the collateral manager can be fired.
Subject to the terms of the collateral management agreement and the CDO's indenture, the collateral manager is responsible for the selection, acquisition, and disposition of the underlying collateral securities. The management agreement and indenture specify limits on the concentration, credit quality, interest, and currency risk of the CDO portfolio. For example, the manager might not be able to invest in securities rated below a certain rating, and is generally required to maintain a minimum collateral weighted-average rating (WAR). Since most tranches are denominated in dollars and pay floating-rate interest based on the London Interbank Offered Rate (LIBOR), indentures also impose limits on the fraction of the portfolio that can be invested in fixed-rate or foreign currency–denominated securities. Subject to these restrictions, the collateral manager bears sole responsibility for the selection of collateral assets.
The collateral management agreement also spells out the manager's compensation in the form of four types of fees: senior, subordinated, incentive, and upfront. Senior and subordinated collateral management fees are calculated as a fraction of the par value of AUM. The senior fees have high priority in the distribution of interest and principal proceeds—they are paid before the AAA-rated notes. Nevertheless, senior fees are not completely insensitive to how the deal performs. Poor performance and defaults on the collateral securities are likely to cause the deal to breach its overcollateralization and interest coverage triggers. Violations of these triggers force the manager to use any subsequent proceeds to pay off the noteholders, in the order of their seniority, leading to a decline in deal AUM. In the extreme, breaching certain triggers can lead to early liquidation, causing the manager to completely lose out on management fees.
Subordinate management fees are paid after distributions to all rated tranches and before any distribution to the equity tranche. These fees are more common and larger in mezzanine deals that are backed by lower rated securities than in high-grade deals backed by highly rated collateral.
Incentive fees are meant to further align the interests of the collateral manager with those of the equity tranche. These fees are similar to carry in hedge funds and private equity funds: the collateral manager receives about 20% of the cash flows available for distribution to the equity tranche, after the equity tranche has realized a hurdle IRR, typically in the 12% to 15% range.
Finally, lump-sum upfront fees are meant to compensate the manager for the expenses of putting the deal together. When present, upfront fees are on the order of one million dollars, or roughly one year of ongoing management fees. These fees reward managers for closing deals irrespective of their subsequent performance.
article.II. Data:
This paper studies ABS CDOs issued during the 2002 to 2007 period. The sample of CDOs is the union of two data sources: Barclays Live and Pershing Square Capital's Open Source Model.13Barclays Live (previously Lehman Live) is a web-based platform that provides Barclays's clients access to Barclays's research and analytics. The platform includes a sample of ABS CDOs with basic information on their structure. Although, in principle, the Barclays Live sample covers deals going back to 1999, one limitation of this sample is that, once liquidated, deals are likely to be removed from the system.
Pershing Square Capital Management's Open Source Model does not suffer from the same survivorship bias as the Barclays Live sample. In early 2008, Pershing posted online a spreadsheet, referred to as the Open Source Model, with data on the underlying collateral securities of 534 CDOs issued during the 2005 to 2007 period.14Since most deals did not start defaulting until late 2007 and early 2008, Pershing's sample provides a snapshot that is relatively free of survivorship bias. The sample may be subject to other selection issues, however, in that it focuses on CDOs that were insured by Ambac and MBIA.
Overall, despite the individual sample selection biases of the two sources, the combined sample covers as many deals as the Intex sample studied by Cordell, Huang, and Williams (2011), and sheds considerable light on the role of CDO collateral managers in the CDO boom and subsequent financial crisis.15
I match the sample of deals issued during the 2002 to 2007 period to Moody's and use its deal classification, which is based on the type and quality of collateral.16Examples of deal types in Moody's data include Cash ABS - High Grade, Hybrid - Mezzanine, Resecuritization - Cash Flow, and CMBS - CRE CDO and RE-REMIC. Following Cordell, Huang, and Williams (2011), I exclude 71 CMBS deals and 27 other deals, primarily Trust Preferred CDOs and Small and Middle Market CLOs, which had unique structural features and invested primarily in non-RMBS collateral. In some of the descriptive summary statistics, I collapse the nine original categories into three: High Grade, Mezzanine, and Resecuritization. TableA.IIreports the volume of different deal types and the mapping between Moody's deal types and the three coarser deal categories. Most of my regression specifications control for deal type-vintage fixed effects; these are based on the original Moody's classification.
Fifty-seven deals are static deals without a collateral manager. These deals are excluded from the analysis.17The final sample, with data on collateral managers and deal performance, consists of 517 CDOs that together are worth $469 billion.
There are 136 unique collateral managers in the sample. I gather information on these managers and their ultimate parents from a multitude of sources: deal prospectuses, S&P pre-sale reports, S&P CDO Manager Focus reports, SEC Form ADV, Capital IQ, and other online sources.
To study the effect of reputational considerations on risk-taking, I would like a measure of the franchise value of each manager's non-CDO businesses. Constructing such a measure is difficult as it requires data not only on AUM, but also on their stickiness and fees. Given the data limitations, I proxy for the importance of non-CDO businesses using the share of CDOs in a manager's AUM, which I refer to asCDO Share. Using a noisy proxy for the franchise value of a manager's other businesses is likely to bias the analyses away from finding any significant results. I measureCDO Shareas of the end of each year using data on AUM and the value of all CDOs closed by a given manager through the end of that year.
Many collateral managers are subsidiaries of larger asset management firms. To capture the parent's franchise value considerations, AUM and the value of CDOs are aggregated up to the level of the ultimate parent. Data on AUM are assembled from deal prospectuses, SEC Form ADV, Capital IQ, and annual reports. Regression specifications relate deal activity during yearttoCDO Shareas of the end of yeart−1.18Overall, I am able to measureCDO Sharefor all but 14 deals.
In addition to measuring each manager'sCDO Share, I classify managers into different types based on the primary business of the collateral manager or its ultimate parent: bank affiliate, CDO manager, hedge fund manager, insurance, investment manager, and other. Although somewhat subjective, this classification provides useful insights into the identity and affiliation of CDO collateral managers.
Deal IRR is an imperfect measure of performance due to a number of data limitations. First, Bloomberg does not have data on cash flows to the commercial paper and super senior tranches.20For deals with such tranches, I calculate IRR by aggregating across tranches for which cash flow data are available in Bloomberg. TableA.IIIshows that the results are robust to excluding deals with commercial paper and super senior tranches. Similarly, Bloomberg does not usually report data on cash flows to the equity tranche. These are unlikely to significantly affect the performance results since the equity tranche is a very small fraction of deal capital structure. Furthermore, TableA.IIIshows that the results hold when measuring performance as the IRR to the AAA-rated tranches or as the loss rate on the underlying collateral.
The final limitation is that the cash flow data stop in December 2015. Ideally, the IRR calculation would include a terminal cash flow equal to the market value of the remaining collateral. Because I do not have the necessary data, I simply set the terminal value to zero. This biases down the average IRR, but my analyses use the cross-sectional variation across similar types of deals issued at the same time. For my results to be affected, it would have to be the case that deals managed by specialized collateral managers generate lower cash flows for the first 7 to 10 years but have more valuable collateral at the end of December 2015. This seems unlikely.21
TableIreports deal-level summary statistics, splitting the sample into three groups: high-grade, mezzanine, and resecuritization.22The table is further divided into three panels, with Panel A reporting deal structure and performance, Panel B showing the characteristics of collateral, and Panel C providing information on management fees.
The average CDO size is around $900 million, but there is significant heterogeneity across deal types with high-grade deals being more than twice as large as mezzanine deals. The share of the deal that is rated AAA averages 93% for high-grade deals and 77% to 78% for mezzanine and resecuritization deals. ABS CDOs perform poorly. The average IRR for high-grade, mezzanine, and resecuritization deals is−24%,−33%, and−37%.
Senior management fees are on the order of 5 to 20 basis points of assets. Although the average fee is twice as high in mezzanine deals (17 basis points) as it is in high-grade deals (seven basis points), in dollar terms, the fees are more comparable. The average high-grade deal pays almost $1 million in senior fees per year, while the average mezzanine deal pays about $1.1 million.
Subordinate fees are relatively unimportant in high-grade deals, averaging around two basis points or about one-quarter of senior fees. In mezzanine deals, however, the magnitudes of senior and subordinate fees are similar. About 28% of high-grade and 18% of mezzanine deals have a lump-sum upfront fee that is paid at deal closing. Finally, 36% of high-grade and 46% of mezzanine deals have an incentive fee.
TableIIreports summary statistics on the collateral managers in the data, with the first panel presenting a breakdown of deal volume by manager type, and the second panel reporting the number of deals, volume, CDO share, and average IRR at the manager level.23There is large heterogeneity in performance across managers, with an interquartile range of 30%.
article.III. Results:
The first prediction of the front men narrative is that investment banks directed deal flow to those collateral managers who were willing to invest in low-quality collateral that the banks were otherwise unable to sell. A plausible alternative is that investors were able to differentiate between good and bad collateral managers, and selected managers with better performing deals. To distinguish between these two alternatives, this section relates changes in a collateral manager's deal volume to the characteristics and performance of the CDOs he closed in the past.
Figure1presents an initial cut of the data. At the end of yeart, managers are sorted into terciles based on the average IRR of the deals they closed during the year. The figure then plots the aggregate change (in dollar and percentage terms) in deal volume between yearstandt+1for managers in different terciles. Note that deal IRR was not observable in real time. The assumption here is that IRR is a noisy proxy for “deal quality” that was observable to some agents but not to the econometricians.
If, similarly to the positive performance-flow relationship in mutual funds and hedge funds, investors directed deal flow to better performing managers, we would expect to see the top performers increasing their deal activity more than poorly performing managers. Figure1shows the opposite pattern. Managers in the bottom and middle terciles attract much more deal volume than managers in the top tercile. During 2005, for example, managers who were in the top tercile of performance in 2004 increased deal volume by $2.7 billion or 23.9%. At the same time, managers in the bottom tercile increased their deal volume by $16.7 billion or 103.2%. Every year during the 2003 to 2006 period, managers in the bottom two terciles experienced larger increases in deal volume both in dollar and percentage terms. These results are inconsistent with investors picking better performing managers, but rather are suggestive of investment banks directing deal flow to managers who were happy to buy low-quality collateral without asking tough questions.
TableIIIprovides more formal statistical evidence. I estimate Poisson regressions of the number of deals closed during semiannual periodton the performance and characteristics of previously closed deals (Wooldridge (2002)).24Using OLS, which does not account for the discrete and nonnegative nature of the dependent variable, generates similar results.
In column (1), the main explanatory variable of interest is the average IRR of deals closed throught−1. The specifications control for the number of previously closed deals, the average number of tranches, the fraction of tranches-rated AAA, the coupon spread on the AAA tranches, and the key characteristics of collateral: its WAR and the shares invested in subprime RMBS and CDOs. All explanatory variables are standardized so that the coefficients represent the effect of a one-standard-deviation change. The IRR coefficient of −0.234 indicates that a one-standard-deviation increase in the average IRR of a manager's past deals is associated with about1−e−0.233=20.8%fewer CDOs closed at timet.
Note again that the interpretation here is not that investors or underwriters observed deal IRR in real time but rather that deal IRR is a proxy for the quality of the collateral manager's deals. In particular, deal IRR is a proxy for the collateral manager's willingness to invest in (i) low-quality collateral underwritten by the CDO's arranger and (ii) collateral that Magnetar Capital bet against. Such behavior was observable to investment banks in real time.
An alternative interpretation is that deals with low ex post IRR looked great at the time and that deal flow was driven by investor demand. To account for this alternative, the specifications control for the fraction of the deal-rated AAA and for the spread on the AAA-rated tranches, two deal characteristics that could be appealing to CDO investors. The results show that neither of these variables is statistically significant. The specifications also control for the collateral WAR and the shares invested in subprime RMBS and CDOs. Thus, the results are not driven by faster growth in deals investing in subprime RMBS, with low IRR proxying for managers investing in low-rated subrprime RMBS.
Column (4) controls for the share of the CDO's portfolio that was invested in the same collateral that Magnetar Capital bet against. The coefficient is positive and highly statistically significant. The result is also economically significant, with a one-standard-deviation increase associated with 39.1% larger deal volume.
Column (5) controls for all explanatory variables simultaneously. The coefficients onSame IB CDO Collateral ShareandMagnetar Collateral Shareretain their magnitude and statistical significance. The coefficient on the IRR of past deals decreases in magnitude from −0.234 to −0.113 and is no longer statistically significant. This reduction in magnitude is consistent with the idea that deal IRR is proxying for the willingness of collateral managers to invest in riskier, lower quality collateral, including collateral that Magnetar bet against and other CDOs underwritten by the deal's arranger.
The results of Figure1and TableIIIsuggest that investment banks directed deal flow to those collateral managers who were willing to invest in low-quality collateral. Which collateral managers were more likely to engage in such a quid pro quo? The front men narrative suggests that managers with low franchise value should be more willing to invest in risky, low-quality collateral in an effort to attract additional AUM.
Figure2shows a binned scatterplot of deal IRR as a function of the collateral manager'sCDO Share.25Observations are first sorted into bins based on theirCDO Share; each dot then represents the average value ofCDO Shareand deal IRR in a given bin. Panel A reports raw values, while Panel B adjusts bothCDO Shareand deal IRR for deal type-vintage fixed effects. Deals of managers with highCDO Sharetend to have significantly lower IRR.
TableIVreports more formal statistical evidence, estimating cross-sectional regressions of deal IRR on the manager'sCDO Share. BecauseCDO Shareis measured at the manager level, the standard errors are adjusted for clustering by manager (Kloek (1981), Moulton (1990)). Column (1) regresses deal IRR on the manager'sCDO Shareand deal-type fixed effects. The coefficient onCDO Shareis negative and highly statistically significant. The economic magnitude is also large: going from 0% to 100%CDO Shareis associated with a 17.3% lower IRR. The standard deviation of IRR is 30%, so this effect represents almost 60% of one standard deviation.
Column (2) controls for deal type-vintage26instead of deal-type fixed effects. Comparing the results in columns (1) and (2) shows that a large part of the negative association betweenCDO Shareand deal performance is due to managers with highCDO Shareclosing more deals at the top of the CDO boom in 2006 to 2007. This finding is consistent with specialized managers being more willing to assemble low-quality deals at the top of the CDO boom. Nevertheless, even within deals closed at the same time and backed by similar collateral, deals of managers with highCDO Shareperform significantly worse. Adjusting for deal type-vintage fixed effects, the standard deviation of IRR is 20%. Going from 0% to 100%CDO Shareis associated with 6.6% lower IRR, or one-third of a standard deviation.
The specifications in columns (1) and (2) of TableIVare deliberately sparse and do not control for many deal characteristics that could be correlated with performance. Controlling for deal characteristics that are within the discretion of the collateral manager and could be determined in part by the manager'sCDO Sharecould hide the effect ofCDO Share. Thus, the goal in TableIVis to first determine whether there is a correlation between the manager'sCDO Shareand deal performance; I later explore in detail which deal characteristics explain the difference in performance. Column (3) adds controls for deal size and number of tranches. These controls have little explanatory power and do not have much effect on the coefficient onCDO Share.
Griffin, Lowery, and Saretto (2014) find that ABS underwritten by more reputable investment banks perform worse than securities underwritten by less reputable investment banks. To ensure that the results are not driven by reputable investment banks working together with specialized collateral managers, column (4) adds underwriter fixed effects. TheR2increases from 0.57 to 0.62, but the coefficient onCDO Shareis not affected. I also try excluding CDOs managed by bank affiliates. This category includes affiliates of both the CDO underwriter and other investment banks. The results, reported in column (5), are similar, with the coefficient onCDO Shareincreasing in magnitude from −0.066 to −0.099.
Finally, column (6) controls for additional manager characteristics that might be correlated withCDO Share: a dummy variable for the manager's first deal, the manager's share of the aggregate deal volume in the year the deal closed, and the log of one plus the manager's cumulative deal volume up to, but not including, the CDO in question. These additional manager characteristics are not statistically significant and do not affect the coefficient on the manager'sCDO Share. Thus, the difference in performance is not driven by first-time managers or by managers who have done a lot of deals in the past or have large market share.
Overall, the results in TableIVshow that deals of specialized managers perform significantly worse than comparable deals of diversified managers. These results are consistent with specialized managers being more willing to respond to the incentives documented in Figure1and TableIII. Given their greater willingness to engage in bad deal-making in an effort to attract AUM, we would expect specialized managers to gain market share over time. Figure3confirms that this is indeed the case. This figure plots the histogram ofCDO Shareduring each year from 2002 until 2007. The distribution starts with most CDOs being managed by diversified managers but shifts over time to highly specialized managers, such as Harding Advisory, who were more willing to invest in collateral that the investment banks wanted to get rid of and that hedge funds like Magnetar wanted to bet against.27
The results of TableIVare consistent with the front men narrative that specialized managers were more willing to invest in lower-quality, riskier collateral in an attempt to earn future deal assignments. However, there are a number of plausible alternative explanations for the negative correlation betweenCDO Shareand deal performance. This section provides evidence against three main alternatives.
The first alternative explanation is thatCDO Sharehappens to be negatively correlated with the manager's direct incentives, that is, management fees and equity stakes, which are the actual drivers of performance. In other words, incentives matter, but it is the direct incentives rather than reputational considerations that determine risk-taking and performance. To address this possibility, columns (2) to (4) of TableVcontrol for management fees and equity stakes. The results show that these factors do not explain the underperformance of specialized managers.
Because management fee data are collected from deal prospectuses, term sheets, and pitchbooks, the sample size is reduced from 517 to 447 deals. Column (1) reports results of the benchmark regression for this smaller sample. Column (2) controls for senior, subordinated, incentive, and upfront fees. Senior and subordinated fees are in basis points, while incentive and upfront fees are dummies for the presence of these fees. Only subordinated fees are statistically significant, with a 10 basis point increase in the subordinated fee associated with 3% higher IRR. Controlling for management fees does not have much effect on the coefficient on the manager'sCDO Share.
Column (3) controls for the manager's equity stake in the CDO. I use deal prospectuses to collect information on the manager's holdings of the equity tranche. About 40% of all deals indicate that the collateral manager is either required or intends to invest in the equity tranche. The coefficient on the dummy variableManager Has Equityis positive 0.051 and statistically significant. Thus, consistent with both signaling and incentive explanations, deals in which collateral managers invest in the equity tranche tend to perform significantly better.28Once again, the coefficient on the manager'sCDO Sharecontinues to be large in magnitude and statistically significant. Column (4) controls for both management fees and the manager's equity stake. The coefficient onCDO Sharedeclines in magnitude to −0.058, but remains statistically significant.
The second alternative explanation is that specialization might be correlated with differences in the ability and experience of the firm's employees. According to this alternative, large diversified managers like BlackRock and PIMCO were able to attract better quality employees who did a better job screening out bad collateral, while smaller and more specialized managers attracted less skilled employees who simply failed to screen out bad collateral that the investment banks wanted to get rid of.29To address this alternative, I construct proxies for the ability and professional experience of each firm's employees. Deal prospectuses list dependence on “key personnel” as one of the key risk factors of investing in CDOs, and almost always include short biographies of the employees considered to be “key personnel.” I use these bios to measure each employee's years of professional experience as of 2004. I then calculate the mean of experience across individual employees.30Following Chevalier and Ellison (1999), I use the selectiveness of the universities from which the firm's employees received their undergraduate degrees as a proxy for their ability. Specifically, I use the 75thpercentile of the SAT scores of the students admitted during the 2001 to 2002 academic year.31Column (5) of TableVreports the results. None of the coefficients on the proxies for employees' ability and experience are statistically significant, and controlling for these proxies does not affect the coefficient onCDO Share. Overall, the results in TableVshow that neither direct incentives nor differences in employees' ability and experience can explain the underperformance of specialized collateral managers.
The third alternative explanation is that specialized managers were simply more optimistic about house prices and about the expected performance of the private-label RMBS that their deals invested in. In effect, this explanation recognizes thatCDO Shareis not exogenous and that both the decision to specialize and deal performance could be driven by an omitted variable—the firm's expectations about the performance of the housing market. If specialized managers were, in fact, more optimistic about the future performance of their CDOs, we would expect them to prefer more performance-sensitive compensation. The evidence in TableVIpoints in the other direction.
TableVIestimates regressions of management fees onCDO Shareand its interaction with a dummy variable for deals that closed at the peak of the CDO boom. Specifically,Peakis equal to one for deals that closed after 2006Q2. This definition of the peak splits the sample into two roughly equal-sized groups. In column (1), the dependent variable is the senior fee. There is no significant variation in senior fees across the specialized versus diversified managers. In column (2), the dependent variable is the subordinated fee. The coefficient onCDO Shareis positive and statistically significant at 5.6%. This result indicates that specialized managers charge three basis points higher subordinated fees. The coefficient on the interaction ofCDO SharewithPeakis negative and highly significant, and indicates that, at the peak of the credit boom, specialized managers charged lower subordinated fees.
In column (3), the dependent variable is a dummy for the presence of an incentive fee. Although the coefficient onCDOShare×Peakis not statistically significant at conventional levels (it has ap-value of 9.7%), it is large in magnitude at −0.24. Since 44% of all deals have an incentive fee, this represents a large effect.
In column (4), the dependent variable is a dummy for an upfront fee. Specialized managers are more than twice as likely to charge an upfront fee (the unconditional probability is 20%), though there is no interaction withPeak. Finally, in column (5), the dependent variable is a dummy for the manager having an equity stake. The coefficients here are not statistically significant.
If specialized managers were more optimistic, we would expect to see them lowering their fees across the board, shifting the mix of fees toward subordinated and incentive fees, and investing in the equity tranches. However, the results of TableVIshow that, instead of expressing their optimism by opting for more performance-sensitive compensation, specialized managers tilt their compensation away from subordinated and incentives fees and toward upfront fees. These changes in compensation mix are not consistent with the idea that managers with highCDO Sharechose to specialize because they were especially optimistic about the performance of securities linked to subprime mortgages.
Assuming that the underperformance of specialized managers is, in fact, driven by their willingness to engage in bad deal-making, what did specialized managers do differently? This section explores differences in deal characteristics and identifies the three main channels through which specialized managers engaged in greater risk-taking. First, they invested in higher yielding securities. Second, they invested in collateral securities that Magnetar Capital bet against. Third, they invested in collateral securities underwritten by the CDO's arranger, with such securities performing worse than observationally similar collateral.
As a starting point, TableVIIexamines the correlation between the manager'sCDO Shareand characteristics of the CDO's collateral, namely, its WAR and spread, age since issuance, fraction underwritten by the CDO's arranger, and share invested in collateral that was included in Magnetar-sponsored deals. All specifications include deal type-vintage fixed effects. A few results are worth noting. First, there are no differences in WAR or weighted-average spread (WAS) on the underlying collateral.32This is not surprising since collateral characteristics such as WAR and WAS fed directly into the credit rating agencies' models.
In column (3), the dependent variable is the weighted-average age of the collateral. This variable measures the time in years between the issuance dates of the collateral securities and the CDO closing date. The coefficient onCDO Shareis negative, indicating that specialized managers invested in collateral from the more recent vintages. Since RMBS issued closer to the peak of the housing boom perform worse, this could help explain the difference in deal performance. Having said that, the economic magnitude is modest. Going from 0% to 100%,CDO Shareis associated with having collateral that is less than a month and a half younger. The average collateral age is around seven months.
In column (4), the dependent variable isSame IB CDO Collateral Share—the fraction of the collateral securities that were themselves CDOs that were underwritten by the investment bank arranging the CDO. The coefficient onCDO Shareis positive and statistically significant. When a manager with 0%CDO Shareinvested in other CDOs, 18% of the securities that he purchased were underwritten by the CDO's arranger. In contrast, when a manager with 100%CDO Shareinvested in other CDOs, 26% of the securities that he purchased were underwritten by the CDO's arranger.
Column (5) reports the fraction of subprime RMBS collateral that was underwritten by the investment bank arranging the CDO. The coefficient onCDO Shareis positive but small in magnitude and not statistically significant. The difference in results between columns (4) and (5) is consistent with the fact that, according to TableIII,Same IB CDO Collateral Sharebut notSame IB Subprime Collateral Sharewas rewarded with additional collateral management assignments. Why would an investment bank reward collateral managers for purchasing other CDOs underwritten by the bank but not for purchasing subprime RMBS underwritten by the same bank? The reason is that they were effectively prevented from doing so by the CDO indentures limiting the fraction of RMBS serviced by a single servicer.33The typical limit is 7.5%, though there can be exceptions for highly rated servicers. Many RMBS underwriters worked with a small set of loan originators and servicers, in some cases affiliates of the underwriter. For example, two-thirds of Lehman Brothers's and one-half of Bear Stearns's subprime RMBS were serviced by their respective subsidiaries: Aurora Loan Services and EMC Mortgage Corp. This means that a CDO underwriter could not ask collateral managers to buy more subprime RMBS underwritten by the bank because doing so would likely violate the single-servicer concentration limit. At the same time, as I show in the next section, CDO underwriters would still want the collateral managers to purchase low-quality collateral from them.
Finally, column (6) shows that specialized managers were more likely to invest in the collateral securities that Magnetar Capital bet against. The magnitudes are once again quite sizable. Going from 0% to 100%,CDO Shareis associated with 5.4% higher Magnetar collateral share. Note that the regression in column (6) excludes deals sponsored by Magnetar. Thus, these results are not about managers with highCDO Sharebeing more likely to work with Magnetar. Rather the results suggest that, even when working with sponsors other than Magnetar, specialized collateral managers with highCDO Shareinvested in the same collateral that Magnetar shorted.
Should CDO investors care that specialized managers bought collateral securities underwritten by the CDO's arranger? Figure4and TableVIIIexamine differences in performance across collateral securities. For each collateral security in the data, I calculate the averageCDO Shareacross all deals that invested in the security. I also create theSame IBdummy that is equal to one for collateral securities that were included in at least one CDO underwritten by the same investment bank. Figure4presents a binned scatterplot of security IRR versusCDO Share. The binned scatterplot groups securities into 20 bins based on theCDO Share. Each dot represents the average value of IRR andCDO Sharefor one such bin. The specifications control for asset class-rating-vintage fixed effects.34Thus, the specifications have a high hurdle to clear as they compare the performance of similar securities issued at the same time and with the same credit rating.
Consistent with the deal-level results in TableIV, Figure4shows a strong negative relationship between security IRR and theCDO Shareof the collateral managers investing in that security. Figure4also suggests that collateral securities that were included in deals arranged by the same underwriter perform significantly worse than comparable securities with the same credit rating and vintage. Furthermore, this underperformance is concentrated among securities held by managers with highCDO Share. In other words, whenever specialized managers invested in collateral securities that were underwritten by the investment bank arranging the CDO, such collateral securities performed especially poorly. For diversified managers, in contrast, the identity of the collateral security's underwriter does not seem to matter much.
TableVIIIprovides more formal statistical evidence, reporting the results of regressions of security IRR onCDO Share, same investment bank dummy, and their interaction. All specifications control for asset class-rating-vintage fixed effects. I also control for the log number of CDOs investing in the security and forMagnetar Collateral, a dummy that is equal to one for securities included in at least one Magnetar-sponsored CDO.35
In column (1), the coefficient on theSame IBdummy is negative and statistically significant, indicating that securities that were included in at least one CDO arranged by the same investment bank tend to perform worse. Column (2) regresses security IRR on the manager'sCDO Share. The coefficient is negative and statistically significant: collateral securities included in deals managed by firms with highCDO Sharetend to perform worse than similar securities with the same rating and vintage.
Column (3) controls for bothSame IBandCDO Share, as well as their interaction. Consistent with Figure4, the coefficients onCDO Shareand its interaction with the dummySame IBare negative and statistically significant. Column (4) controls for the security's coupon spread. Because the sample is restricted to floating-rate securities, the sample size is reduced by about 16%. Securities with larger spreads tend to underperform. A one-percentage-point higher spread is associated with about 3.5% lower IRR.
Finally, columns (5) and (6) split the sample of collateral securities into CDO versus subprime RMBS. For CDO securities in column (5), the manager'sCDO Sharehas a very large negative effect. While the interaction with the dummySame IBis not statistically significant, one cannot reject the hypothesis that it is the same as in the full sample. For subprime RMBS, both the direct effect ofCDO Shareand its interaction withSame IBare negative and statistically significant.
TableIXexamines the correlation between the manager'sCDO Shareand deal structure characteristics, namely, size, number of tranches, share of the deal's tranches rated AAA, WAR of the tranches, coupon spread on the AAA and non-AAA-rated tranches, share of the equity tranche, and overcollateralization of the rated tranches. Three results stand out. First, CDOs of specialized managers did not pay higher coupon spreads on their AAA and non-AAA-rated tranches to compensate investors for the additional risks they were being exposed to.
Second, column (7) indicates that deals of specialized managers had smaller equity tranches. Going from 0% to 100%CDO Shareis associated with a 55 basis points smaller equity tranche. Although this effect might seem small, it is economically meaningful when compared to the mean equity tranche being 306 basis points.
Why would deals of specialized managers have smaller equity tranches? By offering greater leverage, such deals could have been particularly attractive to CDO sponsors like Magnetar that wanted to bet against subprime RMBS. Recall that their strategy consisted of buying the equity tranche while entering into CDS on the underlying collateral or higher rated tranches. The higher current yield on the equity tranche would have helped finance the carry cost of the CDS positions.
Third, column (8) shows that deals of specialized managers had higher overcollateralization, measured as the ratio of the par value of collateral to the par value of the rated tranches. Going from 0% to 100%,CDO Shareis associated with 2.25% higher overcollateralization.
Is there any direct evidence of specialized managers investing in higher yielding securities? Although data on the market prices of ABS are generally difficult to obtain,36prospectuses of some CDOs do report the prices at which the CDOs purchased collateral securities. In my data, there are 18 such deals.37Many of the securities included in these deals were purchased by other CDOs as well. In fact, 348 CDOs done by 107 managers purchased at least one of the securities included in these deals. The sample of securities included in the deals that report transaction prices is obviously not random. TableA.IVshows, however, that there are no significant differences in the observable characteristics of the collateral securities with and without price data.
Columns (1) to (3) report equal-weighted results, while columns (4) to (6) weigh each security by the number of deals investing in that security. The weighting attempts to adjust for the fact thatCDO Shareis measured more precisely for securities included in more deals. Focusing on the results in column (2), which controls for the log number of deals investing in the security, the coefficient onCDO Shareis a highly statistically significant −0.025. This effect is economically significant as it indicates that managers with 100%CDO Sharepurchased collateral that traded at a 2.5% discount relative to par. Note that the magnitude here is broadly in line with the results on the size of the equity tranche and overcollateralization ratio in TableIX: going from 0% to 100%CDO Shareis associated with a 55 basis points smaller equity tranche and 225 basis points higher overcollateralization ratio.38
Overall, this section identifies three main channels through which specialized managers engaged in greater risk-taking. First, they invested in higher yielding securities. Second, they invested in collateral securities that Magnetar Capital bet against. Third, they invested in collateral securities underwritten by the CDO's arranger, with such securities performing significantly worse than observationally similar collateral.
Having examined differences in deal characteristics between specialized and diversified managers, I now reexamine the deal performance regressions of TableIVwhile controlling for deal characteristics that are under the discretion of collateral managers. The idea is to see what fraction of the difference in performance between specialized versus nonspecialized managers can be explained by differences in these deal characteristics. The results are reported in TableXI.
Column (1) reports results of the benchmark regression of deal IRR on the manager'sCDO Shareusing the sample of deals that have all relevant deal characteristics.39Column (2) adds a large set of deal characteristics including the interaction ofCDO SharewithSame IB Collateral Share. The coefficient onCDO Shareis positive and no longer statistically significant, while the interaction withSame IB Collateral Shareis significantly negative. Deals of specialized managers who did not purchase any collateral from the CDO underwriter do not perform any worse than deals of diversified managers. A specialized manager who invested 20% of the portfolio in collateral underwritten by the CDO's arranger underperforms by 9.1%. The shares of CDO and subprime collateral are also negatively correlated with deal IRR.
Column (3) addsMagnetar Collateral Share, which has a large negative effect on deal performance. A 10% increase inMagnetar Collateral Shareis associated with 4.4% lower IRR. Controlling forMagnetar Collateral Sharereduces the magnitude of the coefficients on CDO and subprime collateral shares, making them statistically insignificant. Thus, it is not investment in CDOs or subprime RMBS in general that explains differences in performance but investment in the CDOs and subprime RMBS that Magnetar bet against. Note also that without the interaction ofCDO SharewithSame IB Collateral Share, the coefficient onCDO Shareis negative, about half its magnitude in column (1), and not statistically significant. Thus, it seems thatMagnetar Collateral Shareon its own cannot fully explain the relation betweenCDO Shareand deal performance.
Finally, column (4) controls for all variables at the same time. BothMagnetar Collateral ShareandCDO Share×Same IB Collateral Shareretain their magnitudes and statistical significance. The results in TableXIshed further light on the channels through whichCDO Shareaffects performance. Roughly half of the underperformance of specialized managers is due to investing in collateral that Magnetar Capital bet against. The other part is due to buying collateral from the CDO's underwriter. This collateral does not look different on observable characteristics, but performs significantly worse than observationally similar securities.
This paper argues that the reason diversified asset managers put together better CDOs is that these managers were concerned about the potential negative reputational consequences of assembling risky CDOs on the franchise value of their other businesses. Is there any evidence of such negative reputational consequences in the data? In particular, did investors withdraw capital from the diversified managers who assembled risky CDOs that performed poorly during the crisis?
While the coefficient onManaged CDOsin column (1) is close to zero, its interaction with the institutional fund dummy is negative and statistically significant. The fact that CDO management is associated with outflows only for institutional funds is consistent with institutional investors paying more attention and having a better understanding of CDOs than retail investors. Institutional funds offered by families that also managed ABS CDOs suffered 0.6% larger monthly outflows during the crisis period. Cumulating over the 17-month crisis period, these funds suffered 10.2% larger outflows.
One concern with these specifications is that investors might have withdrawn from the treated funds not because of reputational considerations but because funds offered by families that managed ABS CDOs had greater exposure to CDOs or subprime RMBS. For example, CDO collateral managers could have induced affiliated funds to purchase mezzanine tranches of their CDOs. To address this concern, column (2) restricts the sample to equity funds. Since these funds do not invest in fixed-income securities, they would not have any direct exposure to CDOs and subprime RMBS. Restricting the sample to equity funds generates even stronger results, indicating that the results are not driven by investors withdrawing from mutual funds affiliated with CDO collateral managers because these funds had direct exposure to ABS CDOs.
Column (3) controls for the interaction of returns, fund size, and family size with the institutional fund dummy to further ensure that the interaction term is not driven by institutional investors responding differently to past returns or fund characteristics. For brevity, the interaction terms are not reported. Although some of the interactions with the institutional fund dummy are statistically significant, controlling for these interactions does not affect the coefficient of interest. Indeed, it is actually somewhat larger in magnitude and more statistically significant.
Larger fund families are more likely to manage CDOs. While the specifications in TableXIIcontrol for the log of family TNA, it is possible that the relationship between fund flows and family size is nonlinear and that the interaction ofManaged CDOsand the institutional fund dummy picks up this nonlinear effect. To alleviate this concern, column (4) controls for additional powers—square and cube—of family size, along with their interactions with the institutional fund dummy. For brevity, column (4) omits the coefficients on these additional controls. Controlling for additional powers of family size has no effect on the results.
Column (5) adds nonlinear controls for lagged returns: deciles of returns during each of the past three months along with their interactions with the institutional fund dummy. Compared to column (3), these nonlinear controls for lagged returns increase theR2from 0.130 to 0.135 but have no effect on the coefficient of interest.
Finally, column (6) estimates a placebo regression over the August 2005 to December 2006 period. If the interaction term was capturing some unobservable difference between funds offered by different families, then we should see a similar coefficient in this placebo regression looking at the pre-crisis period. If, on the other hand, the interaction term is, in fact, due to the ex post reputational consequences of managing ABS CDOs, then its coefficient should not be significant during this placebo period. This is exactly what column (6) shows. The coefficient is much smaller in magnitude and is not statistically significant.
Overall, the results in TableXIIsupport the idea that ABS CDO management was associated with negative reputational consequences. Over the course of the crisis period, from August 2007 to December 2008, institutional investors withdrew about 10.2% more capital from funds affiliated with CDO collateral managers than from comparable funds with similar performance.
article.IV. Conclusion:
CDO collateral managers bear ultimate responsibility for the selection of securities in ABS CDOs, securitizations that generated large losses for many financial institutions, jeopardizing their health and helping trigger a full-blown financial crisis. The academic literature to date has largely ignored the role of collateral managers in fueling the CDO boom and contributing to the financial crisis. My paper fills this gap by studying the identity, incentives, and performance of the collateral managers in charge of the ABS CDOs issued during the 2002 to 2007 period.
The paper provides systematic evidence consistent with the front men narrative of the role of collateral managers that has been advanced in legal cases against the collateral managers and in the popular press (Eisinger and Bernstein (2010), Lewis (2010), Koszeg (2012)). According to this narrative, CDO collateral managers invested in low-quality collateral that investment banks were unable to sell to anyone else; in return, the banks directed deal flow toward managers who did not ask tough questions. Not all collateral managers were willing to abdicate their responsibility for the selection of collateral and their duty to investors. My paper suggests that it was specialized managers who were more willing to invest in low-quality collateral as a way to cater to the investment banks and attract deal flow. Concerned that risk-taking in ABS CDOs could have negative reputational consequences for the franchise value of their other businesses, diversified managers invested in better quality collateral but lost market share.
While my results capture cross-sectional differences in incentives and performance across collateral managers, they potentially understate the role of specialized managers in the poor performance of ABS CDOs. Competition from specialized managers could have created a race to the bottom and forced diversified managers to lower their standards as well. State Street agreeing to manage Carina CDO is one anecdotal example of this dynamic. Although the decline in deal performance across vintages is certainly consistent with a race to the bottom, it is difficult to convincingly rule out alternative explanations based on time-series changes in collateral quality.
In addition to contributing to our understanding of the role of CDO collateral managers in the CDO boom and subsequent financial crisis, the paper helps shed light on a potential downside of specialization in asset management. Specialized managers may engage in greater risk-taking than diversified managers because specialized managers do not have any other businesses that could be adversely affected by such risk-taking. By studying the risk-taking incentives of specialized asset managers, the results also contribute to the current policy debate about the systemic risks posed by asset managers. While the debate has focused primarily on the potential for fire sales, my results speak to the ex ante risk-taking incentives of specialized versus large diversified asset managers.
Editors: Bruno Biais, Michael R. Roberts, and Kenneth J. Singleton
